description:
You are acting as a junior backup-administrator who has been asked to archive an existing SQLite database using nothing more than the standard Linux command-line tools that are already available in the container (sqlite3, gzip, sha256sum, etc.).  
The work must be reproducible and fully verifiable by an automated checker, so pay very close attention to the required directory names, the file names, the log names and, most importantly, the **exact format** of every line you write to the log files.

Initial state
1. A database already exists at  
   /home/user/data/sales.db
2. The database contains at least two tables (customers, orders) with a handful of rows each.  
   You do **not** need to modify the data; you only have to back it up.

Required final state
1. A new directory must be created at exactly  
   /home/user/archive/2023-01-15  
   (make parent directories if they do not exist). **Use this hard-coded date; do NOT try to detect today’s date.**
2. Two backup artefacts of the original database must end up inside that directory:  
   a. sales_raw.db    — a byte-for-byte copy of the original database file.  
   b. sales_dump.sql.gz    — a gzip-compressed SQL dump produced with “.dump”.  
      • First create an uncompressed file called sales_dump.sql.  
      • Immediately compress it with gzip so that only the “.gz” file remains in the directory.
3. A SHA-256 manifest must be written to  
   /home/user/archive/2023-01-15/manifest.log  
   The manifest must contain **exactly two lines** (no header), sorted alphabetically by the *file name* (not the path).  
   The line format that the checker will look for is:  
   FILENAME|SHA256|SIZE_BYTES  
   Example (values will obviously be different):  
   sales_dump.sql.gz|58bca2d78c7aa60e8c889f1ff8c1c1dd518448e63734a3595a55af2f4864d3ac|2048  
   sales_raw.db|2f50e6d183d595f94f27dc74ee9801f4e1f6fb0edd44b5f9d67d9f235de7c8e1|11264  
   • There must be **no spaces at all** on either side of the “|” separators.  
   • Each line must end with a single “\n”.  
   • Nothing else (no blank line, no header) may follow the second line.
4. A human-readable summary report must be appended (or created if it does not yet exist) in  
   /home/user/archive/backup_report.txt  
   Add **exactly one line**:  
   2023-01-15 - Backup completed: 2 files, TOTAL_BYTES bytes  
   Replace TOTAL_BYTES with the arithmetic sum of the byte-sizes that you already recorded in the manifest.  
   Example:  
   2023-01-15 - Backup completed: 2 files, 13312 bytes
   • The line must end with “\n” and **nothing else** (no trailing spaces).
5. When everything is finished, the working directory structure must therefore look like this (only relevant entries shown):  
   /home/user/archive/  
   ├── 2023-01-15/  
   │   ├── manifest.log  
   │   ├── sales_dump.sql.gz  
   │   └── sales_raw.db  
   └── backup_report.txt
6. Do not alter or remove any existing files outside the /home/user/archive path.

Validation notes (for your benefit)
• The automated tests will calculate fresh SHA-256 digests of both backup files and compare them with the strings you have written into manifest.log.  
• They will also parse backup_report.txt and verify that TOTAL_BYTES is exactly the sum of the recorded file sizes.  
• Directory names, file names and the formats described above must match **perfectly**, including capitalisation, separators, and new-line placement.

Summary of what you must do (without giving explicit commands)
• Inspect /home/user/data/sales.db to verify that it exists.  
• Create the required /home/user/archive/2023-01-15 directory hierarchy.  
• Make the raw copy and the SQL dump (then compress it).  
• Generate SHA-256 digests and file sizes.  
• Produce manifest.log with the precise format.  
• Append the summary line to backup_report.txt.  
• Confirm that the resulting directory tree matches the specification.

If any step fails, fix it before you consider the task complete; the automated checker is unforgiving about names, ordering and exact log file content.
truth:
Pre-populated artefacts before the agent starts:

1. Path: /home/user/data/sales.db  
   • A valid SQLite 3 database.  
   • Schema:  
     CREATE TABLE customers(id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT, email TEXT);  
     CREATE TABLE orders(id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, customer_id INTEGER, amount REAL, created_at TEXT);  
   • Rows inserted:  
     customers:  
       (1,'Alice','alice@example.com'),  
       (2,'Bob','bob@example.com');  
     orders:  
       (1,1,123.45,'2023-01-10'),  
       (2,2,99.99,'2023-01-11');  

2. No /home/user/archive directory exists at container start.

3. The automated test will:  
   a. Verify that /home/user/archive/2023-01-15/sales_raw.db is byte-for-byte identical to /home/user/data/sales.db.  
   b. Decompress sales_dump.sql.gz, re-import it into a temporary SQLite database and compare schema+data with the original.  
   c. Recalculate SHA-256 digests and compare them with the entries in manifest.log.  
   d. Check that manifest.log follows the strict “FILENAME|SHA256|SIZE_BYTES\n” per line format, filenames alphabetically sorted.  
   e. Confirm that backup_report.txt contains a single, correctly-formatted line for 2023-01-15 and that the TOTAL_BYTES value equals SIZE_BYTES(line1)+SIZE_BYTES(line2).

All directory names, file names and line formats listed above constitute ground-truth information required for verification.
